title: The History of Cartography
body_html:
A map is such a familiar object that it is easy to forget what a strange and remarkable thing it is. To draw a map is to shrink the world onto a flat surface and to decide what to leave in and what to leave out. Every map is therefore a kind of argument about which features of a place matter most. The history of mapmaking, or cartography, is not simply a record of increasing accuracy. It is also a history of changing beliefs about the shape of the world, the purposes maps were meant to serve and the people who made and used them.

The earliest surviving maps are thousands of years old and look nothing like the maps of today. Some of the oldest were scratched onto clay tablets and show small local areas, such as a city and the fields around it. Others were less concerned with geography than with belief, placing a sacred city or a homeland at the centre of the world and arranging everything else around it. These maps were not meant to guide a traveller from one place to another so much as to express how their makers understood their own position in the universe. Distance and direction, as a modern reader would measure them, were often far less important than symbolic meaning.

A great step forward came with the scholars of the ancient Mediterranean world, who began to think about the Earth in mathematical terms. They recognised that the world was a sphere and worked out ways of describing any location by two numbers, giving its position north or south and east or west. This idea, the ancestor of modern latitude and longitude, made it possible in principle to place every point on a single consistent grid. One geographer of the ancient world even compiled a vast list of places with their coordinates, together with instructions for drawing them, so that a map could be reconstructed by anyone who followed his method. Much of this knowledge, however, was lost or neglected for centuries in some regions before being recovered later.

During the medieval period, mapmaking flourished in several parts of the world. Scholars in the Islamic world preserved and extended the mathematical geography of the ancient Mediterranean, producing detailed and comparatively accurate maps and gathering information from travellers who journeyed across vast distances. In Europe, meanwhile, many maps of the time were designed less for navigation than for teaching and contemplation, combining geography with religious and historical ideas. Sailors, by contrast, relied on a different kind of chart that recorded coastlines, harbours and the compass bearings between ports, tools of great practical value to those who made their living on the sea.

The age of long ocean voyages transformed cartography. As ships sailed farther and returned with reports of unfamiliar coasts, mapmakers scrambled to incorporate the new discoveries, and maps became prized and sometimes closely guarded sources of commercial and political power. This period also forced cartographers to confront an old and stubborn problem: how to represent the curved surface of a globe on a flat sheet of paper. Any such representation must distort something, whether shapes, sizes, distances or directions, and no flat map can preserve them all at once. Different solutions, known as projections, were devised to suit different purposes. One famous projection made it easy for sailors to plot a straight course of constant compass bearing, but it did so at the cost of greatly exaggerating the size of regions far from the equator, a distortion that has shaped popular impressions of the world ever since.

For centuries the accuracy of maps was limited by the difficulty of fixing position precisely, especially at sea. Determining how far north or south a ship lay was relatively straightforward, but measuring how far east or west it had travelled long defeated navigators, leading to errors that could wreck vessels and cost lives. The eventual development of reliable methods for finding this second measurement was a milestone that allowed maps of the oceans and coastlines to be drawn with new confidence. Surveying the land, too, grew steadily more systematic, as teams of surveyors measured their way across whole countries to produce maps of unprecedented detail.

Today the tradition continues in forms its early practitioners could scarcely have imagined. Satellites orbiting the Earth gather images and measurements of the whole planet, and digital maps on handheld devices can locate their users to within a few metres and redraw themselves instantly as people move. Yet the essential act remains what it always was: selecting from the endless complexity of the real world a limited set of features and arranging them to tell a useful story. In that sense the humble map still carries out the same task it performed on its earliest clay tablets, even as the tools for making it have been transformed beyond recognition.

title: The Craft of Stained Glass
body_html:
Few art forms depend so completely on their surroundings as stained glass. A painting hangs on a wall and looks much the same whatever the weather, but a stained-glass window is nothing without light passing through it. In the dim interior of a great church, the same window may glow with a soft radiance on a cloudy morning and blaze with colour when the sun breaks through. This dependence on light gives stained glass a changing, almost living quality, and it is one reason the craft has been prized for so many centuries as a way of transforming the inside of a building.

The colours of stained glass do not come from paint applied to the surface. Instead, the glass itself is coloured all the way through while it is still molten, by adding small quantities of certain substances to the mixture. Different additions produce different colours: some metals give shades of blue, others produce greens, reds or yellows. Because the colour is part of the glass itself rather than a coating, it does not fade or peel away as a painted surface might, which helps explain why windows made many hundreds of years ago can still shine as brightly as when they were new. Controlling these colours was a matter of skill and experience, and the exact methods used by early workshops were closely guarded.

Making a window was a demanding process that began not with glass but with a drawing. The designer first prepared a full-size plan showing every line and shape of the finished window. Sheets of coloured glass were then cut into the many small pieces this plan required, a delicate task in an age before modern cutting tools. Details that could not be created simply by shaping the coloured pieces, such as the features of a face or the folds of a garment, were added by painting fine dark lines onto the surface of the glass. The painted glass was then heated in a furnace, or kiln, so that the paint fused permanently into the surface and would not wash away. Only after this were the many pieces ready to be joined together.

The joining was done with strips of a soft metal, lead, which could be bent easily around the edges of each piece. Grooved lengths of lead were fitted between the pieces of glass and then joined at their meeting points, holding the whole design together rather like the lines of a drawing. These dark lines were not merely practical; skilful designers used them as part of the composition, letting them outline figures and separate areas of colour. A completed panel was strengthened and made weatherproof, then fixed into the stone framework of the window opening, often supported by iron bars to help it bear its own weight and resist the wind.

For much of its history, stained glass served above all to tell stories. Many of the people who gazed up at these windows could not read, and the great sequences of glowing images acted as a kind of picture book, illustrating tales and lessons for all to see. A single large window might be divided into many small scenes to be read in order, guiding the viewer through a narrative. In this way the windows were not only beautiful but useful, teaching and reminding a congregation of the stories at the heart of their faith while flooding the interior with coloured light.

The craft rose to extraordinary heights during the medieval period, when new styles of architecture allowed builders to replace heavy stone walls with soaring expanses of glass. Later centuries saw changing tastes, and for a time the art declined in some places, with older techniques half forgotten and many fine windows damaged or destroyed. In the nineteenth century, however, a wave of renewed interest led to a revival, as artists studied surviving medieval windows, rediscovered old methods and created ambitious new works of their own. The craft has continued to evolve since then, with modern makers experimenting with abstract designs and new materials while still relying on the same basic principle their predecessors understood: that coloured glass and passing light together can turn a plain opening in a wall into something that seems almost alive.

title: The Origins of Jazz
body_html:
Jazz is often described as one of the most original art forms to have emerged in modern times, and its history is bound up with the movement, mixing and meeting of peoples. It did not appear suddenly or as the invention of any single person. Instead it grew gradually out of a blend of musical traditions that came together in the southern United States, and above all in the port city of New Orleans, over many years. To understand where jazz came from is to trace how different kinds of music, carried by different communities, flowed into one another until something genuinely new was born.

Several streams fed into this new music. From the songs and rhythms rooted in African traditions came a strong emphasis on rhythm and on the give and take between a leader and a group, in which a phrase sung or played by one performer is answered by others. From European traditions came particular instruments, harmonies and forms, as well as the brass-band music popular at the time. There were also work songs, religious music sung in churches, and a soulful, expressive style of song that dealt frankly with hardship and sorrow. None of these on its own was jazz, but where they overlapped, musicians began to combine their features in fresh ways.

New Orleans was an unusually fertile place for such mixing. It was a busy, diverse port where people of many backgrounds lived side by side, and music was woven into daily life, from dances and parades to funerals and celebrations in the street. Brass bands were especially popular, and second-hand instruments were widely available, so that many people had the chance to learn to play. In this crowded, musical city, performers constantly heard one another and borrowed ideas, and out of this ferment the earliest jazz took shape in the opening years of the twentieth century.

What set the new music apart, more than any particular instrument or tune, was the way it was played. The single most important feature of jazz is improvisation, the art of inventing music on the spot rather than simply performing a piece exactly as written. A jazz musician takes a familiar melody or a set of underlying chords and spins new variations from it, making choices in the moment that will never be repeated in quite the same way. This means that no two performances of the same piece are ever identical, and it places enormous value on the individual voice and creativity of each player. Closely bound up with improvisation is a distinctive rhythmic quality, a lively sense of momentum and swing that makes listeners want to move.

At first this music was passed on almost entirely by ear and by example, learned in performance rather than from written scores, and much of the earliest jazz was never written down at all. Its spread owed a great deal to two developments of the age. One was the phonograph, or sound-recording machine, which for the first time allowed a performance to be captured and heard again far away, so that a style born in one city could reach listeners across a country and beyond. The other was the movement of people. As musicians and their audiences travelled and migrated to other cities, they carried the music with them, and new centres of jazz sprang up far from where it had begun.

As it spread, jazz did not stand still. Musicians in different places adapted it to their own tastes and circumstances, and over the following decades it branched into many contrasting styles, some designed for large dance orchestras and others for small groups of soloists exploring complex ideas. What held these varied styles together was less a fixed sound than a shared attitude: a respect for improvisation, a delight in rhythm, and a willingness to bend and reshape existing material into something personal. This flexibility allowed jazz to keep reinventing itself, absorbing new influences in each generation rather than settling into a single fixed form.

The story of jazz is therefore a story of creativity arising from contact and exchange. It shows how music that might have remained separate can, when communities live and play alongside one another, combine into something none of them could have produced alone. That is why jazz is so often held up not just as a body of music but as an example of how cultures meeting in a shared space can generate art of lasting importance, an art that still carries the traces of the many traditions from which it grew.

title: The Global Spread of the Potato
body_html:
Few foods have travelled so far, or changed the world so profoundly, as the potato. Today it is grown and eaten across the globe and ranks among the most important food crops on Earth, yet for most of human history it was unknown outside a single mountainous region of South America. The story of how this humble tuber spread from those highlands to feed billions of people is a striking example of how the movement of a single plant can reshape societies, economies and even populations far from its home.

The potato was first cultivated thousands of years ago high in the Andes mountains, where farmers grew an astonishing variety of types suited to the cold, thin air and rugged terrain. In this demanding environment, where many grains struggled to grow, the potato thrived. The people of the region developed clever ways of preparing and storing it, including a method of repeatedly freezing the tubers in the cold night air and drying them in the sun to produce a light, long-lasting food that could be kept for years. For these mountain communities the potato was a staple on which daily life depended.

When travellers from Europe reached South America and encountered the plant, they carried specimens back across the ocean. At first, however, the potato was met with suspicion in its new surroundings. It was unfamiliar and grew underground, and some people distrusted it or believed it was unfit to eat. For a considerable time it was treated more as a curiosity to be grown in gardens than as a serious source of food. Only gradually did attitudes change, as its advantages became too great to ignore.

Those advantages were considerable. The potato could produce a large amount of food from a small plot of land, and it grew well in cool, damp climates where the traditional grain crops of the region often did poorly. It was also nutritious, supplying much of what people needed to stay healthy, especially when eaten together with a little milk or other simple foods. For poorer families in particular, a field of potatoes could mean the difference between hunger and sufficiency. As these benefits became clear, the crop spread widely, and in many regions it became a mainstay of the ordinary person's diet, allowing more people to be fed from the same amount of land.

This new abundance had far-reaching effects. Historians have linked the spread of the potato to a period of rapid population growth, as a more reliable food supply allowed more children to survive and communities to expand. In some areas the crop became so central to daily life that a large share of the population came to depend on it almost entirely. This dependence, however, carried a hidden danger. When a population relies too heavily on a single crop, it becomes gravely vulnerable to anything that might destroy that crop, and the potato was not immune to disease.

The risk became tragically clear when a plant disease swept through potato fields in the nineteenth century, causing the tubers to rot in the ground across whole regions. Where people had come to rely on the potato above all other foods, the failure of the harvest brought terrible famine, and its consequences included widespread death and the movement of great numbers of people who left their homes in search of survival elsewhere. The disaster stands as a lasting warning of the dangers of depending too completely on one source of food, a lesson that has influenced thinking about agriculture ever since.

Despite this catastrophe, the potato's global rise continued, and it was carried onward to still more parts of the world, where it took root in a great range of climates and cuisines. In many countries it became so thoroughly woven into everyday cooking that people forgot it had ever been a foreign import, treating it as a natural part of their own traditional diet. Its journey from a remote mountain range to the fields and kitchens of nearly every nation is a reminder that the foods we take for granted often have long and surprising histories, and that a single plant, moving from one part of the world to another, can leave a mark on human life out of all proportion to its modest appearance.

title: Movable Type and the Printing Revolution
body_html:
Before the middle of the fifteenth century, producing a book in Europe was a slow and costly labour. Each copy had to be written out entirely by hand, a task that might occupy a skilled scribe for months. Books were consequently rare and expensive, owned mainly by wealthy institutions and individuals, and the knowledge they contained reached only a small part of society. Within a few decades this situation was transformed by a new technology that made it possible to produce identical copies of a text quickly and in large numbers. The invention of printing with movable type is often counted among the most consequential developments in human history, and its effects reached far beyond the making of books.

Printing itself was not entirely new, for methods of stamping images and text onto paper already existed. In these earlier methods, however, a whole page had to be carved as a single block, an approach that was laborious and inflexible, since a fresh block was needed for every new page and any error meant carving it again. The crucial breakthrough was the idea of movable type: instead of carving each page as a whole, the printer assembled it from a large collection of small, separate pieces, each bearing a single letter. These pieces could be arranged to form any page, then taken apart and rearranged to print something entirely different. A single set of type could therefore be reused endlessly.

Making this work required solving several problems at once. The individual letters had to be produced in great quantities, all of a precisely uniform size so that they would line up evenly, and they had to be hard enough to withstand repeated pressure without wearing down. The solution was to cast the letters from metal, using moulds that allowed many identical copies of each letter to be made rapidly. An ink was also needed that would cling to the metal type rather than running off it, and a press was required to push the paper firmly and evenly against the inked letters. Bringing all these elements together into a single practical system was the achievement that launched the printing revolution.

Once assembled, a page of type could be inked and pressed onto sheet after sheet of paper, producing copies far faster than any scribe. When enough copies of one page had been printed, the type could be broken up and reassembled for the next. The saving in time and effort was enormous. Where a handwritten book might take many months to complete, a printing workshop could turn out hundreds of copies of the same work in a fraction of that time. As printing spread from its birthplace to cities across the continent, the number of books in existence multiplied rapidly, and their price began to fall.

The consequences of this abundance were profound. As books became cheaper and more plentiful, reading was no longer confined to a small elite, and literacy gradually spread more widely through society. Ideas could now travel faster and reach more people than ever before, and it became far harder to suppress a text once many copies of it existed in different places. New thoughts, discoveries and arguments could be shared across great distances and preserved against loss. Printing also helped to standardise written language, since printed books presented the same spellings and forms to readers everywhere, encouraging a more uniform way of writing.

Printing changed the character of knowledge itself. When every copy of a text had been made by hand, small errors inevitably crept in as one scribe copied another, so that no two manuscripts were ever quite the same. Printing produced many identical copies from a single setting of type, which meant that a reader in one place could be confident of reading exactly the same words as a reader far away. This reliability made it easier to refer precisely to a particular passage and to build shared bodies of knowledge on which many people could agree, a quiet but important change in the way information could be trusted and used.

It would be a mistake to imagine that printing brought about these changes instantly or on its own. Its effects unfolded over generations and depended on other conditions, including the availability of affordable paper and a growing appetite for reading. Nevertheless, the arrival of movable type marked a genuine turning point. By breaking the bottleneck that had always limited the copying of texts, it opened the way to a world in which written knowledge could circulate widely and cheaply, laying foundations on which much of later intellectual and social life would be built.

groups.prompt: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage? Write TRUE if the statement agrees, FALSE if it contradicts, or NOT GIVEN if there is no information.

questions.prompt_text: Before printing, each copy of a book in Europe had to be written out by hand.
questions.evidence: Each copy had to be written out entirely by hand, a task that might occupy a skilled scribe for months.

questions.prompt_text: Methods of stamping text onto paper did not exist at all before movable type.
questions.evidence: Printing itself was not entirely new, for methods of stamping images and text onto paper already existed.

questions.prompt_text: The pieces of movable type could be taken apart and reused to print different pages.
questions.evidence: These pieces could be arranged to form any page, then taken apart and rearranged to print something entirely different.

questions.prompt_text: Printing helped to make written language more uniform.
questions.evidence: Printing also helped to standardise written language, since printed books presented the same spellings and forms to readers everywhere

questions.prompt_text: The first printed book was more expensive than a handwritten one.
questions.evidence: ABSENT: not stated or contradicted

questions.prompt_text: Printing brought about all of its effects immediately and by itself.
questions.evidence: It would be a mistake to imagine that printing brought about these changes instantly or on its own. Its effects unfolded over generations

groups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.prompt_text: What was the main drawback of the earlier method of carving a whole page as a single block?
options: It produced copies too quickly
A fresh block was needed for every new page
It could not be inked
It required no skill
questions.evidence: a fresh block was needed for every new page and any error meant carving it again.

questions.prompt_text: What was the crucial breakthrough of movable type?
options: Using paper instead of cloth
Assembling a page from small separate pieces, each bearing a single letter
Printing only images rather than words
Employing more scribes
questions.evidence: instead of carving each page as a whole, the printer assembled it from a large collection of small, separate pieces, each bearing a single letter.

questions.prompt_text: Why were the letters cast from metal?
options: Because metal was cheaper than wood
So that many identical, hard-wearing letters could be made rapidly
Because metal letters were easier to read
So that they would dissolve in the ink
questions.evidence: The solution was to cast the letters from metal, using moulds that allowed many identical copies of each letter to be made rapidly... hard enough to withstand repeated pressure without wearing down.

questions.prompt_text: According to the passage, how did printing change the reliability of texts?
options: It introduced more errors than hand-copying
It produced many identical copies so readers everywhere read the same words
It made texts impossible to refer to precisely
It prevented knowledge from being shared
questions.evidence: Printing produced many identical copies from a single setting of type, which meant that a reader in one place could be confident of reading exactly the same words as a reader far away.

groups.prompt: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt_text: Before printing, who copied books out by hand?
questions.evidence: a task that might occupy a skilled scribe for months.

questions.prompt_text: What device was needed to push the paper firmly against the inked letters?
questions.evidence: a press was required to push the paper firmly and evenly against the inked letters.

questions.prompt_text: As books became cheaper and more plentiful, what gradually spread more widely through society?
questions.evidence: literacy gradually spread more widely through society.
